1: Isaiah 47:1 opens the taunt-song against Babylon with a royal demotion: 'redi veshevi al-afar betulat bat-Bavel = come down and sit in the dust, O virgin daughter of Babylon.' The 'virgin daughter of Babylon' (betulat bat-Bavel = the unviolated/inviolable city, proud and hitherto unconquered) is commanded to descend from her throne to the dust — the posture of the humiliated, those in mourning, and the utterly defeated (Job 2:8: Job sat in the ashes; Lamentations 2:10: 'the elders of Daughter Zion sit on the ground in silence'). 'Sit on the ground without a throne' (shevi la'arets en-kisse bat-Kasdim = sit to the ground without a throne, O daughter of Chaldeans) — the throne (kisse) as the symbol of sovereign power now removed. 'For you will no longer be called tender and delicate' (ki lo tosiphi yiqrehu lakh rakkah va'anugah = for you will no longer be called tender and pampered) — the imperial city's self-image as refined, luxurious, and untouchable is stripped away.
2: 'Take the millstones and grind flour; remove your veil, take off your robe, bare your legs, and wade through the rivers' — the slave's degradation imposed on the queen-city: 'qichi rechayim vetchani qemach = take millstones and grind flour.' The rechayim (millstones = the stone hand-mill for grinding grain, the work assigned to the lowest-ranking slave women: Exodus 11:5: 'from the firstborn of Pharaoh...to the firstborn of the female slave, who is at the handmill'; Numbers 11:8; Matthew 24:41). 'Remove your veil' (galli tsammatekh = uncover/expose your tresses/veil) — the veil in ANE culture was the mark of a free, honorable woman; removing it was the mark of a slave or prostitute (Genesis 24:65: Rebekah covered herself when she saw Isaac; the Assyrian Middle Assyrian Laws specify that slaves and prostitutes were forbidden to veil). 'Bare your legs, and wade through the rivers' (gali-shok ivri neharot = uncover the leg, cross through rivers) — the posture of a slave being led across water by a captor.
3: 'Your nakedness shall be exposed, and your disgrace shall be visible. I will take vengeance, and I will not relent' — the public shaming: 'tiggaleh ervatekhah = your nakedness will be exposed.' The ervah (nakedness/exposure = the most extreme form of public humiliation in ANE culture; the exposure of a conquered city's nakedness in the prophets = the ultimate defeat: Ezekiel 16:37-38; Nahum 3:5-6). 'And your disgrace shall be visible' (vegam tera'eh cherepatekhah = and also your disgrace/reproach will be seen) — the cherepah (reproach/disgrace = the social shame that results from public exposure; the same term as the Rabshakeh's 'reviling' of YHWH in Isaiah 37:3). 'I will take vengeance, and I will not relent' (eqqom naqam velo efga adam = I will take vengeance and I will not intercede with/for a man) — the divine vengeance (naqam = the rightful retribution of a wronged party) executed without mercy. Deuteronomy 32:35: 'Vengeance is mine; I will repay.'
4: 'Our Redeemer — the LORD of hosts is his name — the Holy One of Israel' — the doxological parenthesis: 'go'alenu YHWH tseva'ot shemo = our Redeemer, YHWH of hosts is his name.' The speaker shifts for one verse from YHWH's divine first-person to the community's third-person praise. Go'alenu (our Redeemer = our kinsman-redeemer, from the go'el institution of Lev 25; Ruth 4) — the community celebrating the identity of the one who judges Babylon on their behalf. YHWH tseva'ot (YHWH of hosts = the divine warrior-title; the title of the ark at Shiloh and Jerusalem, 1 Samuel 1:3; 2 Samuel 6:2) combined with qedosh Yisrael (the Holy One of Israel = Isaiah's distinctive title for YHWH throughout the book). The brief doxology interrupts the taunt-song to ensure the reader knows who is judging Babylon: Israel's covenant God, not a superior imperial power.
5: 'Sit in silence and go into darkness, O daughter of the Chaldeans; for you will no longer be called the mistress of kingdoms' — the second demotion command: 'shevi dumam uvo'i bachhoshekh bat-Kasdim = sit in silence and go into the darkness, O daughter of Chaldeans.' The doman (silence = the silence of death and the grave; Psalms 115:17: 'it is not the dead who praise the LORD... those who go down to the place of silence'; Psalms 94:17: 'unless the LORD had been my help, my soul had soon dwelt in silence'). The darkness (choshekh) = the realm of judgment and humiliation contrasted with the light of YHWH's salvation (Isaiah 45:7: 'I form light and create darkness'). 'For you will no longer be called the mistress of kingdoms' (ki lo tosiphi yiqre'u lakh gevirat mamlakhot = for you will no longer be called the mistress of kingdoms) — gevirat (mistress/queen/lady = the feminine of gevir = master/lord; the title of the queen-mother in Judah, 1 Kings 15:13; 2 Kings 10:13). Babylon's imperial queenship ended.
6: 'I was angry with my people; I desecrated my heritage; I handed them over to you; but you showed them no mercy; on the elderly you laid a very heavy yoke' — the divine explanation and the charge against Babylon: 'qatsafti al-ammi chilarti nachalati = I was angry with my people, I profaned my heritage.' The qatsafti (I was angry = the righteous anger of YHWH producing the exile as judgment) and chillalti (I profaned/desecrated = I allowed my inheritance to be treated as common, stripped of its sacred status). 'I handed them over to you' (va'ettem beyadekhah = and I gave them into your hand) — the divine authorization of Babylon's role: Babylon was YHWH's instrument of judgment (Isaiah 10:5-6: 'Assyria, the rod of my anger'). 'But you showed them no mercy' (lo-samit lahem rachamim = you did not set mercies on them) — the charge: Babylon exceeded its mandate. 'On the elderly you laid a very heavy yoke' (al-zaqen hikbadti ullakh me'od = on the elder you made the yoke very heavy) — the abuse of the most vulnerable (elderly people) as the specific evidence of cruelty beyond the divine commission.
7: 'You said, I will be mistress forever, so that you did not take these things to heart or consider how it would end' — Babylon's fatal assumption of permanence: 'vattemeri le'olam ehyeh gevirah = and you said: forever I will be mistress.' The le'olam (forever = the permanent self-perpetuation that only YHWH can claim: Psalms 90:2: 'from everlasting to everlasting you are God'). Babylon claimed for itself the permanence that belongs only to YHWH. 'So that you did not take these things to heart' (lo-samti elleh al-libbakh = you did not set these things to your heart) — the absence of reflection on the contingent nature of Babylon's power. 'Or consider how it would end' (lo zakharti acharitah = you did not remember its end) — the acharit (end/outcome = the outcome that wisdom requires consideration of; Proverbs 14:12: 'there is a way that appears right, but in the end it leads to death'). Babylon's failure of wisdom: not considering how imperial power ends.
8: 'Now therefore hear this, you lover of pleasures, who sit so securely, who say in your heart, I am, and there is no one besides me; I will never sit as a widow or know the loss of children' — Babylon's self-deification: 'veat'ah shim'i zot addinah hayoshevet lavetach = and now hear this, O pleasure-loving one who sits in security.' The adinah (pleasure-lover = the one given to luxury, sensual pleasure, self-indulgence; Jeremiah 51:34 uses similar vocabulary for Babylon). 'Who say in your heart, I am, and there is no one besides me' (ha'omeret bilibah ani ve'afsi od = who says in her heart: I am, and there is nothing besides me) — the 'I am, and there is no other' (ani ve'efsi od) = the exact language YHWH uses of himself in Isaiah 45:5-6, 14, 18, 21-22; Isaiah 46:9. Babylon blasphemously appropriates the divine uniqueness-formula for the imperial city. Revelation 18:7 quotes this verse directly for the end-time Babylon: 'she says in her heart: I sit as queen; I am not a widow; I will never see grief.'
9: 'Both of these things shall come upon you in a moment, in a single day: loss of children and widowhood — they shall come on you in full, despite your many sorceries and the great power of your spells' — the sudden reversal of both claims: 'vetavanah lakh shtei elleh rega beyom echad = and these two things will come upon you in a moment, in a single day.' The two things Babylon claimed would never happen (widowhood and childlessness, v8) come simultaneously and instantly — 'in a moment, in a single day.' 'Despite your many sorceries' (bekhovved keshafayikh me'od = despite the great abundance of your sorceries) — the keshafim (sorceries/witchcraft = the Babylonian magical arts: incantations, protective rituals, divination). 'And the great power of your spells' (uvaottsmat chovareikh me'od = and the great strength of your spellbinders/charm-casters) — the Babylonian professional magical class (chovrim = those who bind spells). Despite the most sophisticated magic system in the ANE, the judgment cannot be averted.
10: 'You trusted in your wickedness; you said, No one sees me; your wisdom and your knowledge led you astray, and you said in your heart, I am, and there is no one besides me' — the three grounds of Babylon's false confidence: (1) 'vattevchechi bera'atekh = you trusted in your wickedness' — the confidence born of a history of successfully doing evil without punishment (cf. Ecclesiastes 8:11: 'when the sentence for a crime is not quickly carried out, people's hearts are filled with schemes to do wrong'); (2) 'amart ein ro'ani = you said: no one sees me' — the claim of divine non-surveillance (Psalms 10:11: 'he hides his face and never sees'; Job 22:13: 'how can God know? Does he judge through such darkness?'); (3) 'chakhmateikh veda'takh hi shevatekha = your wisdom and your knowledge — they have led you astray' — the Babylonian epistemic arrogance: the sophisticated knowledge systems that Babylon prided itself on (astronomy, divination, mathematics) became the mechanism of their self-deception. The second occurrence of 'I am, and there is no one besides me' (v8's formula repeated) = the imperial self-deification as the theological ground of Babylon's judgment.
11: 'But evil shall come upon you, which you will not know how to conjure away; disaster shall fall on you, which you will be powerless to avert; and ruin shall come upon you suddenly, which you never saw coming' — the triple disaster announcement: (1) 'vetavo aleikh ra'ah lo ted'i shacharah = and evil will come upon you, you will not know its dawn/how to avert it' — the ra'ah (evil/disaster) coming without warning or means of avoidance; (2) 'vetippol aleikh hovah lo tukhal kapparoh = and disaster will fall on you, you will not be able to atone for/avert it' — the kapparoh (atonement/covering = the ritual means of covering/removing guilt or danger; Yom Kippur = the Day of Atonement). Babylon's own atonement-rituals will be powerless against YHWH's judgment; (3) 'vetavo aleikh pit'om shoah asher lo-tede'i = and ruin will come upon you suddenly, a desolation you did not know' — the shoah (sudden ruin/disaster; the same root as the Holocaust's name in Hebrew) coming without warning. Cyrus's conquest of Babylon in 539 BCE was indeed remarkably sudden and unexpected.
12: 'Stand by your enchantments and your many sorceries, which you have practiced since your youth; perhaps you will succeed; perhaps you can inspire fear' — the divine sarcasm inviting Babylon to try its magical arts: 'imdiy-na beverakhayikh uvrov kheshafayikh asher yaga'at mine'urayikh = stand now in your enchantments and your many sorceries in which you have labored since your youth.' The me'urayikh (since your youth = Babylon's magical arts were ancient, developed over centuries of Mesopotamian civilization going back to Sumer). 'Perhaps you will succeed; perhaps you can inspire fear' (ulai tukeli ho'il ulai ta'arotsi = perhaps you can prevail; perhaps you will inspire awe/fear) — the sarcastic 'perhaps' (ulai): in the face of YHWH's judgment, the most the magical arts can offer is a 'perhaps.' The entire history of Babylonian divination — the omen series, the astrological texts, the incantation rituals — is dismissed as offering only tentative and ineffectual results against divine action.
13: 'You are worn out by all your advisers; let them stand up and save you — those who divide the sky, who gaze at the stars, who at each new moon predict what will happen to you' — the exhaustion of Babylonian knowledge-systems: 'nile'it berov atsotayikh = you are wearied by the multitude of your counselors.' Three classes of Babylonian religious specialists: (1) 'chotevei shamayim = those who divide/cut the sky' = the astrologers who divided the heavens into zones for omen-interpretation (the Babylonian system divided the sky into three 'roads': the Road of Anu, the Road of Enlil, and the Road of Ea); (2) 'hachozim bakokahvim = those who gaze at the stars' = the astronomical observers who tracked planetary and stellar movements (the Babylonian astronomical cuneiform texts represent the most sophisticated pre-modern astronomy, with accurate records of planetary cycles, eclipses, etc.); (3) 'meyodi'im lechadashim = those who make known by months' = the monthly prognosticators who issued monthly horoscope-style predictions (the Babylonian Almanacs and Goal-Year texts). All three classes of expert are invited to save Babylon — knowing the outcome in advance.
14: 'Look — they are like straw; the fire devours them; they cannot save themselves from the power of the flame. There is no coal for warmth, no fire to sit by' — the judgment by fire: 'hineh hayu kaqash esh serapatam = see, they are like straw; fire has burned them.' The astrologers and diviners compared to qash (straw/chaff = the light, inflammable residue of the threshing floor, the same chaff that the wind blows away in Isaiah 40:24; Isaiah 41:16). 'They cannot save themselves from the power of the flame' (lo-yatsillu et-nafsham miyad lehabah = they cannot deliver their souls from the hand of the flame). The irony: the specialists who were supposed to 'save' Babylon from disaster cannot save themselves. 'There is no coal for warmth, no fire to sit by' (ein-gachelet lehamam ur lagshevet legandeha = there is no coal to warm, no fire to sit before it) — the fire of judgment produces no warmth or benefit, only destruction. Contrast with the idol-maker's fire in Isaiah 44:16 ('Aha, I am warm!').
15: 'Such are those you have dealt with, who have been your trading partners since your youth; they each go off in their own direction; there is no one to save you' — the dissolution of Babylon's commercial network: 'ken hayu lakh asher yaga'at socharayikh mine'urayikh = such are those to you for whom you have labored, your traders from your youth.' The trading partners (sokherayikh = your merchants/traders) who have benefited from Babylon's commercial empire 'since your youth' (mine'urayikh = from your youth = the centuries of Babylonian commercial dominance). 'They each go off in their own direction' (ish ledarkho ta'u = each wanders to his own path) — the commercial abandonment: when Babylon falls, the trading partners scatter, each pursuing their own survival. 'There is no one to save you' (en moshi'ekh = there is no one saving you) — the final verdict: the trading partners, the astrologers, the enchanters — all fail to save. Only YHWH is moshi'a (savior = Isaiah 43:11; Isaiah 45:21), and he is judging Babylon, not protecting it. Revelation 18:9-19 expands this commercial-abandonment motif for the eschatological Babylon.
